channel.title: Open Design Blog
channel.description: Editorial notes on Open Design, agent-native design workflows, BYOK, skills, systems, and community.

item.title: Open Design 0.8.0: everything is a plugin
item.description: Open Design 0.8.0 isn't a release, it's a rebuild. A small plugin engine, a headless-by-default CLI, packaged auto-update on macOS and Windows, and 149 design systems shipped in seven days.

item.title: The layout layer the canvas used to hide
item.description: A community reply on the 0.8.0 preview named the real question behind agent-native design: if the canvas stops being the work unit, how do users still understand layout?

item.title: How to port a Figma workflow into an Open Design plugin
item.description: The 0.8.0-preview thread asks contributors to port old design workflows one plugin at a time. Here is the concrete path for a Figma export, token sync, or brand kit.

item.title: BYOK reality check: 5 things that break in Open Design today
item.description: We promised BYOK as first-class. Five open bug threads from this week — Gemini, DeepSeek, OpenCode, Windows — show where the seams are still rough, and what to use until each fix lands.

item.title: The open-source alternative to Claude Design
item.description: Claude Design is good. It's also closed-source, hosted-only, and bundled with a Claude subscription. Here's the honest read on when to pick it — and when the open-source path wins.

item.title: 31 skills, 72 systems: how the Open Design library works
item.description: A walk through the four primitives that make Open Design composable: skills, systems, adapters, and the daemon. With concrete examples of how a Markdown file becomes a pixel-perfect deliverable.

item.title: BYOK design workflow: run Claude, Codex, or Qwen on your own key
item.description: Most AI design tools quietly add a margin to every token you spend. Open Design takes the opposite stance — bring your own model key, pay the provider directly, and keep full control of where inference runs. Here's how the BYOK layer actually works.

item.title: Why we built Open Design as a skill layer, not a product
item.description: Most AI design tools try to replace the agent already on your laptop. Open Design takes the opposite bet: ship a thin layer of skills, systems, and adapters that turn any coding agent into a design engine — without locking you into a new app.
